video_title: ⚡️Every product of the future will be a living system  — Ronak Malde, Trajectory.ai
gist: Ronak Malde, ex-Windsurf/DeepMind, walks through founding Trajectory.ai, a continual-learning platform that captures real-world user-correction signal to train cheaper/faster domain models (legal, finance, healthcare) and argues every product will become a self-improving 'living system.' He details proprietary techniques (SDPO self-distillation, continuous-LoRA training infra) and customer proof points with Harvey, Clay, Rogo, Decagon, and Mercor.

themes.theme: Continual learning as the next AI unlock

insights.claim: Static models are the core unmet need in AI products: a model used yesterday makes the same mistakes tomorrow and every user correction is wasted, which Trajectory.ai aims to fix via continual learning that compounds product usage into the model.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Signals a new infrastructure category (continual learning) distinct from foundation-model training; watch for which vendors own the user-correction feedback loop.
insights.quote: the model that you used yesterday, it's going to be the same model making the same mistakes tomorrow. All of those corrections you gave it, the edits like in any product is all just being put to waste.

insights.claim: The coding-AI playbook (rapid agent improvement from real usage signal) will repeat across legal, healthcare, and finance, so Trajectory.ai is deliberately starting with customers across multiple verticals to build a domain-agnostic platform.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Implies the value capture that happened in coding agents is about to spread to vertical knowledge-work software; map exposure to legal/finance AI incumbents.
insights.quote: it's very clear now that we're getting that explosion for every single other domain, right? like legal, healthcare, finance, they're all going to be supercharged in the way that coding was for the last few years.

insights.claim: Binary feedback signals (thumbs up/down, accept/reject) are too noisy to train on because users vibe-accept everything; the valuable training signal is the directional content of user edits and corrections after an agent acts.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Reframes what 'RLHF data' is worth; products that capture rich edit-trajectories (not just ratings) hold the defensible data asset.
insights.quote: when is the last time you put a thumbs up thumbs down in cursor or cloud code never, right? And everyone's kind of vibe accepting everything. So it turns out those signals are actually quite noisy.

insights.claim: For high-stakes expert domains like legal, an agent getting 80% of the way there is equivalent to zero value, which makes the user's final 20% correction the most valuable training signal to capture.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Explains why partial-automation metrics mislead in regulated verticals; value accrues to whoever closes the last-mile expert gap.
insights.quote: for a field like legal like getting 80% of the way there is the same thing as zero.

themes.theme: Trajectory.ai platform and customer proof points

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai distills heterogeneous customer usage data into a single 'trajectory' format from which it auto-generates evals, judges, and training environments, enabling self-serve model optimization without forward-deployed engineering.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Removes the services-heavy bottleneck that slows fine-tuning vendors; a scalable, no-forward-deploy model is the key to many-customer leverage.
insights.quote: we essentially take all of the data, all of the expert traces, the agents and distill it into one format which is what we call the trajectory.

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai's onboarding time collapsed from roughly three months for its first customer to training a Harvey model in under a month and a new customer in under a week, evidencing a maturing, repeatable pipeline.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Falling time-to-value is the leading indicator of scalability for an infra startup; supports the claim it can move from 5 to 50 customers.
insights.quote: The first engagement, it took like three months to like set up the entire thing... now we're able to train a model with Harvey in under a month. and then onboarded a new customer and within a week we were able to train a model

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai's named launch partners are Clay, Harvey, Rogo, Decagon, and Mercor, giving it early footholds across AI-native legal, finance, GTM, and support verticals.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: These are high-signal AI-native logos; their adoption validates demand for owned, continually-trained models in regulated knowledge work.
insights.quote: Our our current partners are Clay, Harvey, Rogo, Dakugon, Mor.

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai is a team of 11 positioned explicitly as both a product company and research lab, having hired researchers from OpenAI, Meta Superintelligence, and Amazon AGI plus co-founders from DeepMind and Apple.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Talent density from frontier labs is a moat proxy for a research-heavy infra startup; relevant for competitive and acqui-hire dynamics.
insights.quote: We we've hired an amazing research team from OpenAI, from Meta, Super Intelligence, from Amazon AGI, and then obviously co-founders from from Deep Mind and Apple.

themes.theme: Proprietary research: SDPO and continuous LoRA

insights.claim: Standard RL is broken for continual learning because it collapses rich real-world corrections into a single reward number; Trajectory.ai's self-distillation policy optimization (SDPO) instead gives a teacher privileged hints and matches student log-probs, preserving textual guidance signal.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: A technical edge over GRPO-style training for production feedback; if it generalizes, it lowers the data needed to improve deployed agents.
insights.quote: RL it's still taking all of this kind of useful information from the real world like I mentioned all the corrections and everything and putting it into just one number which is really broken.

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai claims SDPO is the first scaling of academic self-distillation to real-world use cases, showing faster convergence than GRPO-style training on the Apex agents benchmark and robustness to off-policy production data.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Faster convergence on off-policy data is the practical requirement for training from live product logs; key technical differentiator to verify.
insights.quote: It's been done in a lot of academic cases but no one's actually been able to scale it up to real world use cases.

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai open-sourced a continual-learning training stack ('continuous LoRA') with Berkeley's SkyRL lab and Anyscale that runs concurrent training and sampling pools, cutting wall-clock time roughly in half for two concurrent jobs and scaling toward 16 concurrent runs.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Continual learning needs always-on, parallel training infra unlike batch slurm jobs; open-sourcing it seeds an ecosystem and signals where the bottleneck is.
insights.quote: even as you scale up experiments, so like two concurrent jobs, we're able to cut the wall clock time in half.

insights.claim: Continual learning reframes model training as a production distributed-systems problem (scheduling, preemption, concurrent dynamic workloads) rather than a one-off research job, requiring an engineering-mindset training stack.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Points to a new infra layer (always-on training schedulers) as a likely investable bottleneck as continual learning scales.
insights.quote: training is becoming more production right uh like the end goal of trajectory is that every company can be kicking off their own training runs like continuously learning updating

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai partnered with Harvey and Nvidia to train Nemotron 3 Super to the Pareto frontier on legal workflows, choosing it for sovereign/regulated-industry needs and a drastically cheaper, faster footprint than frontier models at scale.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Validates Nvidia's open-model strategy and cheap inference economics for high-volume regulated deployments; bullish read-through for Nemotron adoption.
insights.quote: we partnered with Harvey and Nvidia in order to train Neatron 3 super uh in order to get to the paro frontier.

insights.claim: Western open-source base models still trail trillion-parameter Chinese models (Kimi, GLM, DeepSeek) at large scale, but Nvidia's investment has made the 12B Nemotron meaningfully better than OpenAI's year-old GPT-OSS, pointing to a coming wave of Western open models.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Tracks the closing open-source gap that matters for legal/finance/healthcare where sovereignty rules out Chinese weights; watch Nvidia-backed open models.
insights.quote: I think America or the the western world has some work to do still like obviously having one trillion parameter models like Kimmy like and and amazing models like GLM and Deepseek. Uh I don't think we're quite there yet for that size of model.

themes.theme: Product roadmap and the living-system thesis

insights.claim: Trajectory.ai's roadmap moves from owning the model layer toward giving customers (e.g. PMs) direct, legible control to inspect agent weaknesses and wake up to a smarter model, then to pure observability-driven continual learning for tech incumbents and eventually Fortune 500 like Walmart.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Maps the TAM expansion path from AI-native startups to enterprise; the observability-to-training abstraction is the lever for mass-market continual learning.
insights.quote: I want to have the customer say like a PM should that's like managing an agent product should be able to say hey okay here's where my agent is really good here are the areas where it's still failing

insights.claim: Full continual learning requires jointly optimizing model, harness, skills, and memory layers together, an area Ronak says no one has solved and which holds open research questions on training harness and model in tandem.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Flags the unsolved frontier; whoever cracks joint harness-plus-model optimization could own the continual-learning stack.
insights.quote: no one really knows what it means to train the har like optimize the harness and the model together right and I think there's very interesting research there

themes.theme: Windsurf, DeepMind, and the data-signal lesson

insights.claim: Windsurf's edge came from post-training its own SWE-1 foundation model on rich agent and user-edit signal to beat the frontier, a 'build the model around the product' loop Ronak credits to Windsurf, Cursor Composer, and Claude Code.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Establishes the precedent that owning product-usage data plus a self-trained model is the compounding moat now being generalized across verticals.
insights.quote: we were able to post train on all of that user signal uh and now beat the frontier.

insights.claim: Google/DeepMind acquired Windsurf for $2 billion specifically because owning frontier models is no longer enough without seeing real-world product usage signal, a logic Ronak says is being repeated by Cursor and xAI.
insights.speaker: Ronak Malde, founder of Trajectory.ai
insights.so_what: Confirms strategic value of product-usage data to model labs; read-through for why labs keep acquiring or partnering with applied AI products.
insights.quote: they're also starting to realize that you need to not just own really stellar models, but you also need to know what users are actually doing with products and like seeing all of that signal
